30:54 computers store numbers I'll give a
30:57 simplified explan

31:03 while humans can count from 0 to 9 so
31:07 there's sort of a mismatch between our
31:09 number systems for integers this is not
31:12 a problem the computer can just use a
31:15 bunch of zeros and ones to store it
31:19 however for decimal numbers or floats
31:22 sometimes the computer can't store the
31:24 number properly for example here's how

31:30 0.2 using only zeros and ones at the top
31:35 is what the computer stores at the

31:47 actually reaches it and this is why we

31:52 with floats because computers can't

32:02 example computers can store

311:59 error The Last Detail we're going to
312:01 learn about objects is a really
312:03 important detail and that is objects are
312:07 references to demonstrate let's create a
312:10 new variable with an object so
312:14 const object one and make it equal to an
312:20 object inside this object let's have a
312:23 property
312:25 message colon and the string
312:29 hello so you might think that when we
312:32 create an object like this we create a
312:35 value that has other values inside and
312:38 we save the whole thing and a variable
312:41 however this actually works a little
312:43 differently when we create this object
312:47 the actual value is created somewhere in
312:49 the computer's memory and this variable
312:53 actually just gets a reference that
312:55 points to that location in the
312:57 computer's memory this is like a
312:59 shortcut on your computer a shortcut
313:02 points to where the actual file or
313:04 folder is so there's no way to actually
313:07 see this reference but just just know
313:10 that this is a reference and the object
313:12 is somewhere else in the computer's
313:14 memory so the reason it works this way
313:17 is to make the language more efficient
313:20 for example below this line we can
313:23 create a variable const and call it
313:27 object 2 and make it equal to object
313:33 one so this code doesn't actually make a
313:37 copy of the object it this makes a copy
313:40 of the reference that points to the
313:43 object so this makes a language more
313:45 efficient because if we had a really big
313:48 object here it's not good to copy the
313:51 entire object over and over again
313:54 instead Javas just copies the reference
313:57 which is much faster this is called copy
314:00 by
314:02 reference now that we learned that
314:04 objects are actually references let's
314:07 learn some interesting behaviors of
314:10 references so at the bottom here we're
314:13 going to change the property message in
314:16 this object we can do that by typing the
314:19 object object
314:23 1. message property and make it equal to
314:27 something else like the string good
314:32 job and now if we
314:35 console. log object one
314:41 and
314:42 save notice that we were able to change
314:45 the message property even though we
314:47 created object one with const and that's
314:51 because this variable just contains a
314:53 reference that points to the object so
314:56 const prevents us from changing the
314:59 reference in the variable but it doesn't
315:01 prevent us from reaching into the
315:03 reference and changing the object that
315:05 it's pointing to so just be careful when
315:08 you're working with with objects even
315:11 though we use const we can still change
315:13 the values inside the object another
315:17 interesting behavior is that on this
315:19 line we're not actually copying the
315:22 object into this variable we're copying
315:25 the reference so object two actually has
315:28 the same reference as object one they
315:31 actually point to the same object in the
315:33 computer's
315:35 memory so at the bottom if we type
315:38 console
315:41 log object 2 and save you'll notice that
315:47 for object two the message property was
315:51 also changed even though we only changed
315:53 object one the last interesting behavior
315:57 is that we can't compare objects
315:59 directly for example let's go to the
316:03 bottom and we'll create a new object
316:06 using
316:08 const object 3 and we'll make it equal
316:12 to an object and then inside we're going
316:16 to have these same values as object one
316:19 so we'll have the property
316:22 message and the value is the string good
316:29 job so now let's try comparing object 3
316:33 to object one so here we'll type console
316:39 do
316:40 log and check if object 3 is equal to
316:47 object
316:48 one if we
316:51 save it will tell us that these two
316:53 objects are not equal to each other even
316:56 though they have the same values inside
316:60 and that's because remember objects are
317:02 references so object 3 also contains a
317:06 reference and this code compares the
317:09 references and not the values inside the
317:12 object so because object 3 is a
317:15 different reference than object one
317:18 that's why this code results in
317:21 false if we
317:22 did
317:24 console. log and we check if object 2 is
317:30 equal to object
317:33 1 object two has the same reference as
317:37 object one it points to the the same
317:39 object in the computer's memory so if we
317:43 save this will tell us that these two
317:46 variables have the same
317:48 reference so just remember that when
317:50 you're comparing two variables that

748:04 in this lesson we're going to learn a
748:06 feature of JavaScript called modules
748:09 which is a better way to organize our
748:11 code so first let's take a look at a
748:14 problem that we have in our current code
748:17 we're going to go to our files here and
748:20 we're going to open amazon.
748:22 HTML and then we're going to scroll to
748:25 the bottom of this file to our script
748:29 tags so right now we're using three
748:32 script tags to run all of our
748:34 JavaScript so this runs all the code in
748:37 cart. JS and then it runs all the code
748:40 in products. JS and finally it runs all
748:43 the code in amazon. JS so this is
748:47 basically the same thing as combining
748:50 all the files together into one big file
748:53 and then running all of our code however
748:56 there's a big problem with using script
748:58 tags like this and that problem is it
749:01 can cause naming conflicts so in our
749:05 situation here we created a variable
749:07 named cart in cart. Js
749:11 this means we can't use the variable
749:13 name cart in any of our other files so
749:16 for example if we went to amazon.
749:19 JS and then we scroll to the top
749:22 here and let's say that we try to create
749:25 another variable named cart so at the
749:28 top we type const cart equals square
749:32 brackets and
749:35 save and now if we go back to our
749:38 project you'll notice that the website
749:40 stops working and if we rightclick
749:44 inspect and open the
749:47 console it will tell us that we got an
749:49 error and it says that cart has already
749:52 been declared so this is happening
749:54 because we created a variable called
749:57 cart in cart. JS and then later in
749:60 amazon. JS we created another variable
750:03 named cart now we can't have two
750:05 variables with the same name in
750:07 JavaScript and this causes an error and
750:10 it causes our website to break so this
750:12 is called a naming conflict when we have
750:15 two variables with the same
750:17 name so the big problem with script tags
750:20 is if we go back to our
750:25 code you can see here that it's really
750:28 hard to tell which variables have been
750:30 created in other files and it's really
750:33 easy to accidentally create a naming
750:35 conflict like this so that's the problem
750:39 with using script tags like this and
750:41 running our code one file at a time it's
750:44 very easy to get naming
750:46 conflicts so now we're going to learn
750:48 how to solve this problem using a
750:51 feature of JavaScript called
750:53 modules so a module basically contains a
750:56 variable inside a file so now this
750:60 variable is not going to cause conflicts
751:02 with anything outside the file for
751:06 example in our project we created a
751:08 variable called cart and we want to
751:11 contain this variable inside the cart.
751:14 JS file so that it doesn't conflict with
751:17 the cart variable inside the amazon. JS
751:20 file so we can do this using a module so
751:24 now let's learn how to create our first
751:27 module we're going to go back to amazon.
751:31 HTML and then here to create a module
751:34 we're simply going to delete this line
751:37 of code that uses a script tag to load
751:39 cart. JS so we'll delete it and
751:44 save and you'll see that the website is
751:46 working again so how does this work when
751:50 we use a script tag it will run all the
751:53 code inside cart. JS so this code will
751:57 create a card variable and this will
751:59 cause a naming Conflict by removing the
752:02 script tag we're no longer running the
752:04 code inside cart. JS so we're no longer
752:08 creating a cart variable and it will no
752:10 longer cause a naming
752:14 conflict so to create a module we simply
752:17 do two steps number one we create a file

897:12 regenerate all the HTML this technique
897:15 is called
897:17 MVC MVC stands for model view controller
897:21 and is a popular technique in software
897:24 engineering in MVC we split our code
897:27 into three parts the first part is the
897:29 model this is all the code that saves
897:32 and manages the
897:34 data so in our project all the code in
897:38 this data folder is called the model
897:40 because this code saves and manages our
897:44 data the second part of MVC is the view
897:48 this is code that takes the data and
897:50 displays it on the page so in our
897:53 project if we open checkout. JS and
897:56 scroll to the top here we have code that
898:00 takes our data and then generates all
898:03 the HTML so this code is called the
898:06 view the last part of MVC is the
898:09 controller so this runs some code when
898:12 we interact with the
898:14 page so in our project at the
898:17 bottom these event listeners here would
898:20 be called the controller because they do
898:23 something when we interact with the page
898:25 or with the
898:27 view so in MVC we divide our code Lo
898:30 into these three parts and then they
898:33 interact with each other in a
898:35 loop first we use the model to generate
898:38 the
898:40 view so in our code that's what we did
898:43 here we took the model or the data which
898:46 is our cart and then we generated the
898:49 HTML or the view then when we interact
898:52 with the view it will run the
898:56 controller so that's what we did at the
898:59 bottom here
899:01 when we interact with the page we run
899:03 some
899:04 code then the controller will update the
899:09 model so that's what we did here inside
899:12 the controller we just updated our data
899:15 using this
899:17 function and finally we use the updated
899:20 model to regenerate The
899:23 View so that's what this code does it
899:26 takes the updated data and then
899:28 regenerates all the HTM ml or
899:31 regenerates The
899:33 View so that's how MVC Works instead of
899:36 updating the page directly with the Dom
899:39 we just update our data and then
899:41 regenerate all the
899:44 HTML so we use MVC because doing this
899:49 makes sure that the page always matches
899:51 the
899:52 data so MVC is known as a design pattern
899:56 it's a way to organize and design our
899:59 code in the future you might use a
900:02 technology called a JavaScript framework
900:04 to build websites many JavaScript
900:06 Frameworks are based on MVC so it's

1070:37 objectoriented
1070:38 programming objectoriented programming
1070:41 tries to represent the real
1070:44 world in the real world a cart is a
1070:48 physical object object so in
1070:50 objectoriented programming we represent
1070:53 this physical object as a digital object
1070:56 or in this case a JavaScript
1070:58 object a physical cart can have products
1071:02 inside so our JavaScript object can also
1071:05 have products
1071:08 inside a physical cart also has actions
1071:11 we can take to modify the cart like we
1071:14 can add a product or remove a product
1071:20 our JavaScript object also has actions
1071:23 we can take to modify the cart like the
1071:27 add to cart function or the remove from
1071:30 cart
1071:31 function so as you can see
1071:34 objectoriented programming tries to
1071:37 represent the real world we take a
1071:40 physical object like a cart and
1071:42 represent it as a digital object like a
1071:45 JavaScript
1071:47 object and some people feel feel that
1071:49 this makes the code more intuitive or
1071:52 easier to

1173:48 we
